31: 32: 33: 34: 35: --- 36: 37: Chatwoot is the modern, open-source, and self-hosted customer support platform designed to help businesses deliver exceptional customer support experience. Built for scale and flexibility, Chatwoot gives you full control over your customer data while providing powerful tools to manage conversations across channels. 38: 39: ### ✨ Captain – AI Agent for Support 40: 41: Supercharge your support with Captain, Chatwoot’s AI agent. Captain helps automate responses, handle common queries, and reduce agent workload—ensuring customers get instant, accurate answers. With Captain, your team can focus on complex conversations while routine questions are resolved automatically. Read more about Captain [here](https://chwt.app/captain-docs). 42: 43: ### 💬 Omnichannel Support Desk 44: 45: Chatwoot centralizes all customer conversations into one powerful inbox, no matter where your customers reach out from. It supports live chat on your website, email, Facebook, Instagram, Twitter, WhatsApp, Telegram, Line, SMS etc. 46: 47: ### 📚 Help center portal 48: 49: Publish help articles, FAQs, and guides through the built-in Help Center Portal. Enable customers to find answers on their own, reduce repetitive queries, and keep your support team focused on more complex issues. 50: 51: ### 🗂️ Other features 52: 53: #### Collaboration & Productivity 54: 55: - Private Notes and @mentions for internal team discussions. 56: - Labels to organize and categorize conversations. 57: - Keyboard Shortcuts and a Command Bar for quick navigation. 58: - Canned Responses to reply faster to frequently asked questions. 59: - Auto-Assignment to route conversations based on agent availability. 60: - Multi-lingual Support to serve customers in multiple languages. 61: - Custom Views and Filters for better inbox organization. 62: - Business Hours and Auto-Responders to manage response expectations. 63: - Teams and Automation tools for scaling support workflows. 64: - Agent Capacity Management to balance workload across the team. 65: 66: #### Customer Data & Segmentation 67: - Contact Management with profiles and interaction history. 68: - Contact Segments and Notes for targeted communication. 69: - Campaigns to proactively engage customers. 70: - Custom Attributes for storing additional customer data. 71: - Pre-Chat Forms to collect user information before starting conversations. 72: 73: #### Integrations 74: - Slack Integration to manage conversations directly from Slack. 75: - Dialogflow Integration for chatbot automation. 76: - Dashboard Apps to embed internal tools within Chatwoot. 77: - Shopify Integration to view and manage customer orders right within Chatwoot. 78: - Use Google Translate to translate messages from your customers in realtime. 79: - Create and manage Linear tickets within Chatwoot. 80: 81: #### Reports & Insights 82: - Live View of ongoing conversations for real-time monitoring. 83: - Conversation, Agent, Inbox, Label, and Team Reports for operational visibility. 84: - CSAT Reports to measure customer satisfaction. 85: - Downloadable Reports for offline analysis and reporting. 86: 87: 88: ## Documentation 89: 90: Detailed documentation is available at [chatwoot.com/help-center](https://www.chatwoot.com/help-center). 91: 92: ## Translation process 93: 94: The translation process for Chatwoot web and mobile app is managed at [https://translate.chatwoot.com](https://translate.chatwoot.com) using Crowdin. Please read the [translation guide](https://www.chatwoot.com/docs/contributing/translating-chatwoot-to-your-language) for contributing to Chatwoot. 95: 96: ## Branching model 97: 98: We use the [git-flow](https://nvie.com/posts/a-successful-git-branching-model/) branching model. The base branch is `develop`. 99: If you are looking for a stable version, please use the `master` or tags labelled as `v1.x.x`. 100: 101: ## Deployment 102: 103: ### Heroku one-click deploy 104: 105: Deploying Chatwoot to Heroku is a breeze. It's as simple as clicking this button: 106: 107: [![Deploy](https://www.herokucdn.com/deploy/button.svg)](https://heroku.com/deploy?template=https://github.com/chatwoot/chatwoot/tree/master) 108: 109: Follow this [link](https://www.chatwoot.com/docs/environment-variables) to understand setting the correct environment variables for the app to work with all the features. There might be breakages if you do not set the relevant environment variables. 110: 111: 112: ### DigitalOcean 1-Click Kubernetes deployment 113: 114: Chatwoot now supports 1-Click deployment to DigitalOcean as a kubernetes app. 115: 116: 117: 118: 119: 120: ### Other deployment options 121: 122: For other supported options, checkout our [deployment page](https://chatwoot.com/deploy). 123: 124: ## Security 125: 126: Looking to report a vulnerability? Please refer our [SECURITY.md](./SECURITY.md) file. 127: 128: ## Community 129: 130: If you need help or just want to hang out, come, say hi on our [Discord](https://discord.gg/cJXdrwS) server. 131: 132: ## Contributors 133: 134: Thanks goes to all these [wonderful people](https://www.chatwoot.com/docs/contributors): 135: 136: 137: 138: 139: *Chatwoot* © 2017-2026, Chatwoot Inc - Released under the MIT License. 212:   def dispatch_reauthorization_event(reauthorization_required)
213:     return if ENV['ENABLE_INBOX_EVENTS'].blank?
214: 
215:     changed_attributes = { reauthorization_required: [!reauthorization_required, reauthorization_required] }
216:     Rails.configuration.dispatcher.dispatch(INBOX_UPDATED, Time.zone.now, inbox: self, changed_attributes: changed_attributes)

232:   def apply_sanitization_rules(name)
233:     name.gsub(/[\\<>@"!#$%&*+=?^`{|}~:;()]/, '')        # Remove forbidden chars
234:         .gsub(/[\x00-\x1F\x7F]/, ' ')                   # Replace control chars with spaces
235:         .gsub(/\A[[:punct:]]+|[[:punct:]]+\z/, '')      # Remove leading/trailing punctuation
236:         .gsub(/\s+/, ' ')                               # Normalize spaces
237:         .strip
238:   end
239: 
240:   def display_name_from_email
241:     channel.email.split('@').first.parameterize.titleize
242:   end
243: 
244:   def dispatch_create_event
245:     return if ENV['ENABLE_INBOX_EVENTS'].blank?
246: 
247:     Rails.configuration.dispatcher.dispatch(INBOX_CREATED, Time.zone.now, inbox: self)
248:   end
249: 
250:   def dispatch_update_event
251:     return if ENV['ENABLE_INBOX_EVENTS'].blank?
252: 
253:     Rails.configuration.dispatcher.dispatch(INBOX_UPDATED, Time.zone.now, inbox: self, changed_attributes: previous_changes)
254:   end
255: 
256:   def ensure_valid_max_assignment_limit
257:     # overridden in enterprise/app/models/enterprise/inbox.rb
258:   end
259: 
260:   def delete_round_robin_agents
261:     ::AutoAssignment::InboxRoundRobinService.new(inbox: self).clear_queue
262:   end
263: 
264:   def check_channel_type?
265:     ['Channel::Email', 'Channel::Api', 'Channel::WebWidget'].include?(channel_type)
266:   end
267: end

19:   end

21:   def weekly_schedule
22:     working_hours.sort_by(&:day_of_week).map do |wh|
23:       {
24:         'day_of_week' => wh.day_of_week,
25:         'closed_all_day' => wh.closed_all_day,
26:         'open_hour' => wh.open_hour,
27:         'open_minutes' => wh.open_minutes,
28:         'close_hour' => wh.close_hour,
29:         'close_minutes' => wh.close_minutes,
30:         'open_all_day' => wh.open_all_day
31:       }
32:     end
33:   end
34: 
35:   # accepts an array of hashes similiar to the format of weekly_schedule
36:   #  [
37:   #    { "day_of_week"=>1,
38:   #      "closed_all_day"=>false,
39:   #      "open_hour"=>9,
40:   #      "open_minutes"=>0,
41:   #      "close_hour"=>17,
42:   #      "close_minutes"=>0,
43:   #      "open_all_day=>false" },...]
44:   def update_working_hours(params)
45:     ActiveRecord::Base.transaction do
46:       params.each do |working_hour|
47:         working_hours.find_by(day_of_week: working_hour['day_of_week']).update(working_hour.slice(*OFFISABLE_ATTRS))
48:       end
49:     end
50:   end
51:

2:   queue_as :low
3: 
4:   BATCH_SIZE = 5_000
5: 
6:   def perform(object, user = nil, ip = nil)
7:     # Pre-purge heavy associations for large objects to avoid
8:     # timeouts & race conditions due to destroy_async fan-out.
9:     purge_heavy_associations(object)
10:     object.destroy!
11:     process_post_deletion_tasks(object, user, ip)
12:   end
13: 
14:   def process_post_deletion_tasks(object, user, ip); end
15: 
16:   private
17: 
18:   def heavy_associations
19:     {
20:       Account => %i[conversations contacts inboxes reporting_events],
21:       Inbox => %i[conversations contact_inboxes reporting_events]
22:     }.freeze
23:   end
24: 
25:   def purge_heavy_associations(object)
26:     klass = heavy_associations.keys.find { |k| object.is_a?(k) }
27:     return unless klass
28: 
29:     heavy_associations[klass].each do |assoc|
30:       next unless object.respond_to?(assoc)
31: 
32:       batch_destroy(object.public_send(assoc))
33:     end
34:   end
35: 
36:   def batch_destroy(relation)
37:     relation.find_in_batches(batch_size: BATCH_SIZE) do |batch|
38:       batch.each(&:destroy!)
39:     end
40:   end
41: end

1: class AutoAssignment::AssignmentJob < ApplicationJob
2:   queue_as :default
3: 
4:   IN_FLIGHT_TTL = 5.minutes
5: 
6:   # Coalesce per inbox: at most one AssignmentJob per inbox is in-flight
7:   # (queued or running) at any time. The marker carries a token so a job only
8:   # releases its own claim (a newer job may have taken it after a TTL lapse).
9:   def self.enqueue_for_inbox(inbox_id)
10:     key = format(::Redis::Alfred::AUTO_ASSIGNMENT_IN_FLIGHT_KEY, inbox_id: inbox_id)
11:     token = SecureRandom.uuid
12:     return false unless ::Redis::Alfred.set(key, token, nx: true, ex: IN_FLIGHT_TTL)
13: 
14:     return true if perform_later(inbox_id: inbox_id, token: token)
15: 
16:     # Enqueue was halted; release our own claim so the inbox isn't gated until the TTL.
17:     ::Redis::Alfred.delete_if_equals(key, token)
18:     false
19:   rescue StandardError
20:     # Enqueue raised after we claimed the gate; release our own claim, then re-raise.
21:     ::Redis::Alfred.delete_if_equals(key, token)
22:     raise
23:   end
24: 
25:   def perform(inbox_id:, token: nil)
26:     inbox = Inbox.find_by(id: inbox_id)
27:     return unless inbox
28: 
29:     service = AutoAssignment::AssignmentService.new(inbox: inbox)
30:     assigned_count = service.perform_bulk_assignment(limit: bulk_assignment_limit)
31:     Rails.logger.info "Assigned #{assigned_count} conversations for inbox #{inbox.id}"
32:   rescue StandardError => e
33:     Rails.logger.error "Bulk assignment failed for inbox #{inbox_id}: #{e.message}"
34:     raise e if Rails.env.test?
35:   ensure
36:     release_in_flight(inbox_id, token)
37:   end
38: 
39:   private
40: 
41:   # Release the in-flight marker only if we still own it. The atomic
42:   # compare-and-delete ensures a job whose TTL lapsed can't delete a newer
43:   # job's claim. Tokenless (pre-deploy) jobs never claimed a key, so skip.
44:   def release_in_flight(inbox_id, token)
45:     return if token.nil?
46: 
47:     key = format(::Redis::Alfred::AUTO_ASSIGNMENT_IN_FLIGHT_KEY, inbox_id: inbox_id)
48:     ::Redis::Alfred.delete_if_equals(key, token)
49:   end
